facts.text: George Washington was the first president of the United States and served from 1789 to 1797.
John Adams was the first president to live in the White House.
Thomas Jefferson oversaw the Louisiana Purchase in 1803, greatly expanding the nation.
James Madison is often called the 'Father of the Constitution.'
Abraham Lincoln led the country through the Civil War and is remembered for preserving the Union and ending slavery.
Lincoln was assassinated on April 14, 1865, at Ford's Theatre.
Theodore Roosevelt was the youngest person to become president, taking office at age 42.
William Howard Taft was both a U.S. president and later Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.
Franklin D. Roosevelt is the only president elected to four terms.
Harry Truman was president when World War II ended and when the Marshall Plan began.
John F. Kennedy inspired a generation with his New Frontier message before his assassination in 1963.
Lyndon B. Johnson signed major civil-rights legislation including the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
Richard Nixon became the only president to resign from office after the Watergate scandal.
Jimmy Carter later became known globally for his humanitarian and diplomatic work after the presidency.
Ronald Reagan was a former actor before becoming the 40th president of the United States.
Barack Obama became the first Black president in U.S. history when he took office in 2009.
Presidents are both heads of state and heads of government in the American constitutional system.
The White House has served as the official residence of U.S. presidents since 1800.
George Washington became the first president of the United States in 1789.
He set many early precedents for the presidency and the federal government.
George Washington is one of the most important topics associated with U.S. Presidents.
Books, documentaries, and classrooms that cover U.S. Presidents frequently highlight George Washington.
Learning about George Washington helps explain a major part of U.S. Presidents.
For many readers, George Washington stands out as one of the defining subjects in U.S. Presidents.
The story of George Washington is closely tied to the broader themes of U.S. Presidents.
George Washington is often used to introduce students to the history and significance of U.S. Presidents.
Many surveys of U.S. Presidents return again and again to the importance of George Washington.
From public memory to scholarship, George Washington plays a notable role in how people understand U.S. Presidents.
One reason U.S. Presidents remains so compelling is the lasting significance of George Washington.
The legacy of George Washington continues to shape how U.S. Presidents is discussed today.
